artifacts.content:
Hi Carla,

I’m hiding in the office for five minutes while the preschoolers are at circle and trying to catch you before your shift.

First, THANK YOU again for that $12,000 lifeline you handed me at our kitchen table on June 30. I still see us with the stupid yellow legal pad, writing out that IOU and you insisting on only 3% interest. I hate that it’s December and I haven’t paid a dime back yet. Between the surprise HVAC repair here in August and a really slow enrollment month in October, I’ve been juggling bills just to make payroll and keep Maple Creek happy about the security deposit bump.

On top of that, the HELOC from Summit Credit Union that Daniel and I took out on the house for the build‑out (the $95,000 one) is now two months behind on the interest‑only payments. I haven’t told Mom and Dad, obviously, and I’ve only given Daniel the very sanitized version. If enrollment doesn’t fill those two toddler spots by February, I honestly don’t know how we catch up.

And then there’s the lease. I keep hearing Michael Torres’ voice from that 2/14/25 email about “Personal Exposure in Maple Creek Lease.” That personal guarantee and the whole clause where Maple Creek Properties can accelerate three years of rent if anything ever happens with our use here… I still can’t believe I signed that. If the city ever pulled the plug or we had to close, it would be a nuclear bomb on our finances.

All of this is swirling in my head while I’m trying to plan winter family night and remember to send Lena with soccer snacks.

Can we do coffee Sunday after lunch at Mom and Dad’s? I need your nurse brain and your little‑sister honesty.

Love,
Sarah

Hi Em,

I’m typing this from the couch with Mateo snoring on my lap and my laptop balanced on a pillow, so forgive any typos.

Ever since that three‑day strep throat knock‑out in October, things have felt…different. I finally went to see an internal medicine doc at Froedtert & the Medical College in Wauwatosa — Dr. Melissa Chang — because the chest tightness and insomnia weren’t going away. She actually wrote “rule out anxiety disorder / burnout” in my chart. Her suggestion was to give it until early 2026 and then consider a low‑dose SSRI if I’m still waking up at 3 a.m. running staffing scenarios in my head.

It’s not helping that I’m still haunted by that informal warning email from Karen O’Leary back on 8/19/25 — subject line “Informal Warning – Ratio Compliance Concern, Willow Brook ELC, 8/19/25.” All because I stepped out of the toddler room to take a call from Maple Creek about the lease and we were out of ratio for like five minutes. I keep replaying it and wondering what else I’m missing.

And then there’s Mark Ellison, the neighbor from 1463 West Maple. When I met him in the Brookfield City Hall lobby on 11/6 before that pre‑application meeting, he straight‑up told me he’s thinking about hiring an attorney to oppose us unless I agree in writing to limit outdoor play to two 30‑minute sessions. I’ve been rewriting our outdoor schedule and noise language ever since.

Some nights I stare at the document on my screen and think about that draft resignation letter I saved on my Google Drive in September — the one literally titled “If I Walk Away.” I had just spent the week covering in classrooms, chasing down float staff, and wading through emails, and I spiraled into looking up director jobs at KinderCare in Milwaukee.

Anyway, how do you keep your brain from chewing on this stuff 24/7? Do you actually manage not to check email after 9:30 like you tell your staff?

Hugs,
Sarah

Sarah: Hey, you still up?

Daniel: Yeah, just finished looking at tomorrow’s job schedule. What’s up?

Sarah: My brain won’t shut off again. Same chest tightness as last week.

Daniel: Did Dr. Chang say that was anything to worry about?

Sarah: At the Froedtert internal med clinic in Wauwatosa she basically said it’s stress until proven otherwise. Wrote “rule out anxiety disorder / burnout” and told me if it’s still like this by early 2026 we should talk about a low‑dose SSRI.

Daniel: I hate that this place is doing that to you.

Sarah: It’s not just the center, it’s everything. We’re two months behind on the Summit Credit Union HELOC we took on the house for the build‑out. I’m still juggling the $12k I borrowed from Carla on June 30 for payroll and the Maple Creek security bump. And Michael Torres’ email from 2/14 about the personal guarantee in the Maple Creek lease keeps replaying in my head — the whole “they can accelerate three years of rent” thing if something goes wrong.

Daniel: We’ll figure it out. I can pick up more overtime.

Sarah: I don’t want you living at job sites. And Mom’s stuff is adding to it. Fridays are already tight because I have to leave the center by 2:30 to get her to Aurora West Allis for those Parkinson’s check‑ins with Dr. Nandakumar and PT.

Daniel: I know. You’re carrying too much.

Sarah: Some nights I open that “If I Walk Away” letter I drafted in September and wonder what would happen if I just applied at that KinderCare in Milwaukee.

Daniel: One step at a time. Let’s get through this month. We’ll talk to your accountant, maybe refinance, and you call Dr. Chang back if the chest stuff gets worse.

Sarah: Okay. I’ll try to sleep. Thanks for listening.

Daniel: Always. Love you.

Sarah: Love you too.

Friday To‑Dos – 2025-12-12

CENTER
- Recheck staff schedule for next week. Build in extra float coverage so we never repeat that 8/19/25 ratio mess when Karen O’Leary dropped in and sent the “Informal Warning – Ratio Compliance Concern, Willow Brook ELC, 8/19/25” email. Make sure at least one floater is always assigned to toddler room when I’m anywhere near the phone.
- Draft reminder to teachers about outdoor play expectations: no whistles, no amplified music, redirect kids if “screaming just to scream” starts. Mark Ellison from 1463 West Maple is already edgy after that City Hall lobby conversation on 11/6 where he threatened to get a lawyer to fight us unless I cap outdoor time to two 30‑minute sessions.
- Update parent newsletter with playground schedule and parking etiquette. Emphasize staff parking in back, keep front spots open.

FAMILY/HEALTH
- Call Froedtert & the Medical College internal medicine clinic in Wauwatosa to see if Dr. Melissa Chang has any earlier follow‑up slots. Ask about non‑medication ideas for the chest tightness/insomnia before we talk SSRIs in early 2026.
- Block off calendar: leave center by 2:30 to get Mom to Aurora West Allis Medical Center for her Parkinson’s follow‑up with Dr. Priya Nandakumar + PT. Bring updated med list and ask about stiffness she mentioned Sunday.
- Double‑check Lena’s soccer schedule; practice moved indoors next week.

MONEY
- Email accountant about: (1) being two months behind on the Summit Credit Union HELOC payments from the $95k we used for the build‑out; (2) how to treat the $12,000 personal loan from Carla on 6/30/25 in the books; (3) any way to build a small emergency fund for surprise HVAC‑type hits.
- Review Michael Torres’ 2/14/25 email (“Re: Personal Exposure in Maple Creek Lease”) and highlight any deadlines or triggers tied to that personal guarantee/accelerated rent clause.

PERSONAL
- 20 minutes tonight to journal about the “If I Walk Away” letter and whether I still feel that desperate, or if that was just September exhaustion talking.
- No email after 9:30 p.m. Try.

Title: Mom – Neuro + PT at Aurora West Allis

Time: Friday, December 19, 2025, 3:15–5:00 p.m.
Location: Aurora West Allis Medical Center – Neurology clinic + PT

Details:

Leave Willow Brook by 2:30 p.m. so I’m not sprinting out the door. Ask Mariah to cover front office and make sure ratios are solid so we don’t have another situation like Karen O’Leary’s 8/19/25 surprise visit and “Informal Warning – Ratio Compliance Concern” email.

Pick up Mom (Ana) at her apartment in Wauwatosa around 2:45. She moves slower on cold days, so plan extra time getting from the car into the hospital. Appointment is with Dr. Priya Nandakumar, the neurologist who diagnosed her early‑stage Parkinson’s at Aurora West Allis back in July 2024. After neuro, she has a short physical therapy session in the same building.

Questions to ask:
- Her tremor seems a little worse in the evenings; is that normal progression or meds?
- Are Friday afternoon PT sessions enough, or should we add something closer to her place?
- How realistic is it for me to keep doing these Friday 3 p.m. slots long‑term with center hours?

Bring:
- Updated list of Mom’s meds.
- Notebook with Dr. Nandakumar’s previous recommendations.

Note to self: This is why Fridays feel so tight. Between leaving the center early for Mom’s appointments and trying to keep up with everything there (enrollment, neighbor complaints, money stress), no wonder Dr. Chang at Froedtert is worried about burnout. Be kind to yourself tonight — easy dinner, no email after 9:30.

metadata.title: Mom – Neuro + PT at Aurora West Allis

artifacts.content:
Hi Mark,

Thanks again for taking the time to meet with me in the Brookfield City Hall lobby on 11/6 before that pre‑meeting. I’ve been thinking a lot about what you shared regarding playground noise and parking along West Maple.

I wanted to follow up with some specifics about what we’re already doing and what we’re changing.

Outdoor play: We’ve tightened our schedule so we rarely have more than 12–16 children outside at once, and we’re keeping individual sessions to 30–40 minutes. I heard your concern about wanting no more than two 30‑minute sessions per day; while I do need to balance that with licensing requirements and kids’ need for fresh air, we’re clustering groups so there are longer quiet stretches between play blocks. We also retrained staff on tone and volume outside — no whistles, no amplified music, and active redirection if kids are “screaming just to scream.”

Noise buffering: The fenced area on the south side already has mature evergreens and a 6‑foot solid wood fence; we’re planning to add some additional shrubs and soft surfacing in the spring to help absorb sound. I’m also spending more time out there personally to monitor.

Parking: All staff are now required to park in the rear portion of the lot so the front spaces closest to the building stay open for families and for your neighbors’ patients and clients. We’ve added reminders in our parent handbook and regular emails asking families to keep drop‑off and pick‑up under 10 minutes.

I know you mentioned possibly talking with an attorney if you didn’t feel heard. My hope is that by keeping communication open and making concrete adjustments, we can be good neighbors and avoid that route.

If you’re open to it, I’d be happy to walk the site with you one afternoon so you can see the layout and talk through any other ideas.

Best,
Sarah Martinez
Owner/Center Director
Willow Brook Early Learning Center, LLC

metadata.subject: Follow-up on playground and parking concerns

artifacts.content:
Private reflection – saved to personal Drive

Title: “If I Walk Away” – reality check
Date: 2025-09-28

Tonight after getting the kids in bed, I reopened the draft resignation letter I titled “If I Walk Away.” It’s still sitting in my Google Drive, next to grocery lists and Lena’s school projects, but it feels like a live grenade.

This month has been brutal: covering in classrooms because two staff quit, reworking schedules after Karen O’Leary’s 8/19 “Informal Warning – Ratio Compliance Concern, Willow Brook ELC, 8/19/25” email, and trying to smooth things over with Mark Ellison from 1463 West Maple after his emails about playground noise and parking overflow. Then that conversation in the City Hall lobby where he said he might hire a lawyer to fight us unless I cap outdoor play to two 30‑minute sessions — it all piles up.

Financially, I’m scared. We pulled that $95,000 HELOC from Summit Credit Union on the house to pay for the build‑out, and now we’re sliding behind on payments. I still owe Carla the $12,000 we wrote out by hand on June 30 at my kitchen table to cover payroll and Maple Creek’s security deposit increase. And hanging over everything is the lease with Maple Creek Properties — Michael Torres’ 2/14 email about the personal guarantee and accelerated three years of rent if anything goes sideways keeps me up at night.

On top of that, Mom’s early‑stage Parkinson’s diagnosis from Dr. Priya Nandakumar at Aurora West Allis last July means Friday afternoons are a blur of leaving the center early and navigating hospital parking garages. No wonder Dr. Melissa Chang at Froedtert is talking about burnout and maybe an SSRI if I’m still this fried by early 2026.

Writing that resignation letter in September felt like the only way to breathe. Tonight, I’m not sure I actually want to walk away. I just want this to feel sustainable.

Hello Team,

I hope this email finds you well. As we wrap up the year, I wanted to share some exciting updates and important reminders about our beloved Willow Brook Early Learning Center.

Firstly, I'm thrilled to announce that we are in the final stages of securing a Conditional Use Permit to officially operate our child day care center at our current location, 1458 West Maple Avenue, Suite B, Brookfield, WI 53045. This step is crucial for us to continue providing high-quality care and learning experiences to our community. Our center, nestled in a charming commercial building alongside a dental office and an accounting firm, is truly becoming a hub for neighborhood families!

In line with our commitment to maintaining a safe and organized environment, I have attached our updated parking and circulation plan. It highlights our designated short-term parking spaces for parents and ensures smooth drop-off and pick-up during our peak hours of 7:15 AM to 8:30 AM. Remember, staff should continue parking in the rear section of the lot to leave front spaces available for families and other tenants.

Our outdoor play area remains a favorite feature, thanks to the careful planning that minimizes noise impact on our neighbors. The 3,000-square-foot playground on the south side of the building, buffered by evergreen trees and a solid wood fence, offers a perfect spot for our children to engage in safe, supervised play.

I also want to acknowledge the hard work and dedication each of you puts into making Willow Brook a warm and welcoming place. With a maximum of 18 employees on-site at any given time, it's essential that we continue to support each other and maintain the high standards we've set. Please ensure that all your certifications are up-to-date—we have a color-coded spreadsheet in the office for your reference.

Lastly, as we continue to grow, maintaining open communication with our property managers is key. Should you need to contact them for any facility-related concerns, you can reach them at (414) 555-0198 or via email at info@maplecreekprops.com. Their alternate contact details are (414) 555-2743 and leasing@maplecreekprops.com.

Thank you all for being an integral part of our journey. I am incredibly grateful for your hard work and dedication. Here's to more milestones and memorable moments in the coming year!

Warm regards,

Sarah Martinez
Owner & Center Director
Willow Brook Early Learning Center, LLC
Phone: (414) 555-7321 
Email: sarah.martinez@willowbrookelc.com
